passages.title: Should We Use Tablet Computers in the Classroom?
passages.text:
A local primary school is considering using tablet computers in all classrooms from next year. The following text has been written by a teacher at the school.

Should we use tablet computers in the classroom?

The use of tablet computers (or simply 'tablets') in the classroom has become an increasingly popular option in many primary schools across the country, including many in our local area. It is important that we consider this opportunity, but we must also be aware of the drawbacks.

On the one hand, there are many positives to using tablets in the classroom. Firstly, tablets could improve productivity during the school day. Because of their familiarity with computers and mobile phones, many of our students can type faster than they can write by hand; therefore, using tablets in the classroom could reduce the time it takes for them to complete tasks. Additionally, there are endless tablet functions which could provide interesting teaching activities. For example, we could play interactive games to teach maths, or make simple animations to show how magnets behave. Alternatively, students could take photos outdoors and then use them as prompts for writing tasks.

However, we must also consider the potential problems with introducing tablets to our classrooms. Some students may become distracted by their tablets, and play with the device instead of focusing on their work. Another issue is that, whilst many students can use tablets already, many of our teachers are not as competent and would need to be trained. We may also have problems with the tablets breaking, which would be both frustrating and expensive to fix. This is in addition to the cost of buying the tablets in the first place.

Overall, despite these potential downsides, it seems that using tablets in the classroom would be a wonderful opportunity for students and teachers at our school. There are costs involved, but it would almost certainly prove to be a worthwhile investment.

passages.title: When Pigs Fly
passages.text:
A medieval court. The KING sits on his throne on a raised platform, centre, surveying the room. LORDS and LADIES are seated around the perimeter. A PAGE stands below, facing him. Behind the PAGE stands LORDS.

PAGE:Your Excellency, the final visitor today is Mr Jeremy Dumpling, representing the kingdom's pig farmers. (To Jeremy) You may approach His Royal Highness.
JEREMY:(stepping forward nervously, then kneeling) Your Royal Highness, I have erm... Well I have come here today to draw your attention to what may, or might, be considered as something of a national inconvenience.
KING:(yawning) Yes, yes, go on then. What exactly is the nature of this inconvenience?
JEREMY:Well, Your Wonderfulness, it's about the pigs, you see.
KING:I had guessed as much. What exactly is the problem with these pigs?
JEREMY:Well, Your Majesty, to the past few months, very mysteriously and without explanation, the pigs in the kingdom have; one by one, quite independently…
KING:Spit it out, man!
JEREMY:The pigs have started flying.
KING:(catching his breath) I beg your pardon, Sire, but I really am quite serious!
JEREMY:(struggling to be heard over din) I beg your pardon, Sire, but I really am quite serious!
KING:Pigs flying! My good man, you cannot possibly be serious! You are quite the comedian, Mr Dumpling. Do be sure to contact my Head of Staff about joining our delightful troupe of court jesters. Until then, good day to you. Page, you may escort Mr Dumpling from the castle.
PAGE:(The PAGE helps Jeremy up and starts walking him briskly away.)
JEREMY:(firmly) Young man, you are trying my patience. Shall I call my guards? If you would just allow me to show you one pig, Your Joyfulness…
KING:Fine. I will permit you to show me one pig, Mr Dumpling. One pig only.
JEREMY:(kneeling in front of the king) Thank you, Sire. You will not regret it.

passages.title: The Lion and the Hare
passages.text:
There was once a brave young lion. He was strong and swift. His coat shone in the sunlight and his mane glinted like a crown on his head. When he roared, all the other animals trembled. He strutted around the plains, king of all he saw.

For months now, there had been no rain. The waterholes where animals had gathered to drink had all dried up. Most of the animals had moved north, where the grass was greener and there was more rain.

One afternoon, the lion awoke with a stomach as empty as his cave. There were usually many animals grazing around for him to hunt and eat. Herds of zebras, gazelles and antelopes often grazed nearby. Wildebeests and impalas would wander through. Today was different. Today, the plains seemed empty.

Suddenly, from out of the corner of his eye, the lion saw the grass twitching. He turned and saw the tips of two long, brown ears. A hare, he thought. How perfect! An easy meal if ever there was one. Small, maybe, but enough for today.

The lion licked his lips and started to move towards the hare. Then he stopped. The hare would make an easy meal, but if he could catch an antelope, he would have a feast! His mouth watering, the lion made his decision. Quick as a flash, he leapt out from his hiding place and chased after the antelope. But the lion, weak and tired from lack of food, was soon left far behind.

Slowly, he turned and wandered back to the spot where he had watched the hare nibbling the grass. It was nowhere to be found.

The lion was left with nothing. Just a very empty stomach, and the feeling that maybe he should have been content with what he had to start with.

passages.title: Come to Chester
passages.text:
Give yourself 10 minutes to read the text and answer the questions.

Come to Chester…

The historic city of Chester is the perfect place for a weekend break, with something for the whole family to enjoy.

Roman Ruins

The city is perhaps most famous for its Roman ruins. The Romans settled in Chester almost two thousand years ago and evidence of this can still be seen today. You can walk along the impressive city walls, as the soldiers once did to watch for invaders, and appreciate the stunning views of the city. You can find out more about the Roman settlement by visiting the Dewa Roman Experience, where lunch is complimentary. You and the kids can see and smell what a street might have been like, and even try on some armour. You can also visit the famous amphitheatre, where Roman soldiers once fought and trained.

Historic City Centre

Chester is home to the Victorian-made Eastgate clock, arguably the second most photographed clock in the world (after Big Ben in London). A short detour from there will take you to the Rows. These timber-frame medieval buildings are unique to Chester and house a huge range of shops from high street stores to exclusive boutiques. When you're ready to relax, take a stroll around the beautiful medieval cathedral, or stop for a picnic in Grosvenor Park.

Great Sports Facilities

If you fancy something energetic, the Northgate Arena has superb facilities, including several swimming pools (with water slides) and a spacious gym.

Fantastic Restaurants

At the end of a fun-filled day, why not enjoy a meal at one of Chester's many restaurants? There's food for all tastes and ages, from traditional pub fare to exotic Thai curries. You'll be spoilt for choice!

passages.title: The Wright Brothers
passages.text:
Orville and Wilbur Wright were the inventors of the first successful aeroplane. This is the story of how the brothers took to the skies.

The Wright Brothers

The Wright brothers had always loved building things. As children, they would repair toys and even try building their own. When they grew up, they turned their interest in mechanics into a career. In 1892, inspired by the fashion for cycling, they opened a bike shop together, where they designed and built bicycles.

A few years later, in 1896, the brothers' attention was caught by stories of men attempting to build flying machines. Adventurous inventors were trying to design gliders that would allow men to fly. However, these gliders were difficult to design and one man even died when his glider crashed. Orville and Wilbur believed that their experience with bicycles could help them to build a safe, successful glider. They soon began spending their time testing gliders of their own.

By 1902, the brothers believed they had perfected a system of steering and controlling a glider. The next step was to add power. The mechanic from their bike shop, Charlie Taylor, helped them to build an engine for their plane. The next year, they were ready to test their flying machine.

On December 17th 1903, with some apprehension, Orville and Wilbur tested their plane for the first time. The first exhibition, staying in the air for almost a minute. It does not seem impressive by today's standards, but the triumphant brothers had made the history books with their first powered flight.

Over the next few years, the brothers performed in their flying machine in America and France before huge crowds. People could hardly believe their eyes. Finally, man's dream of flight had come true.

passages.title: The Beast Below
passages.text:
1Under the busy city street,

2Under the hustling, bustling feet,

3Beneath the river and the park,

4In the daylight, in the dark,

5Below the city, in the tunnels deep

6A metal dragon lies asleep.

7With lightning speed and clashing claws,

8From hidden lair come thunderous roars.

9It waits and starts its new attack.

10Slides and glides along the track.

11With painted body, bright eyes of light

12See deep into the inky night.

13It snakes afar by night and day

14To stations that are far away.

15At every stop it opens wide

16To let its passengers inside.

17Then idles, waiting for some more

18To squeeze themselves within the door.

19At times, alone, it rattles by

20With hissing breath and piercing cry,

21It sweeps along on metal rails,

22With whispering sigh of twisting trails,

23Under the hustling, bustling feet,

24Searching down the endless lanes

25For those who like to ride in trains.

passages.title: How Camels Survive in the Desert
passages.text:
The world's deserts are some of the harshest environments on the planet. Temperatures can reach over 40°C in the day, there are few available water sources, and there can be violent sandstorms. Camels have had to develop special characteristics to survive in these challenging conditions.

Perhaps the camel's most famous feature is its hump. This is actually a very important adaptation. If the camel cannot find any food in the desert, it can live off the fat stored in its hump instead.

Desert plants are good sources of water and nutrients for camels, but they are often spiky to try to prevent animals from eating them. In response, camels have developed thick lips and a leathery mouth so that they can eat the plants without feeling pain.

Sand is everywhere in the desert. It can be blown fiercely by the winds, so camels have to be specially adapted to cope with this. To help them keep sand out of their bodies, camels have very long eye lashes and lots of ear hairs to catch the grains. They also have the ability to close their nostrils against the raging sands.

Because the desert is a very dry environment, with few available water sources, its inhabitants need to be efficient with their water management. Camels have very few sweat glands, allowing them to sweat less, retain the water they do consume and stay hydrated.

Despite the high temperatures, lack of food and water, and other challenges in the desert, camels are so well adapted that they are found in deserts across Africa, Asia and Australia. In fact, they have become essential for humans living in these locations, who use camels to transport food, water (and themselves) across the challenging terrains.

passages.title: The Swiss Family Robinson
passages.text:
An extract from The Swiss Family Robinson, by Johann David Wyss. The narrator, William Robinson, is the ship's company crowded into the lifeboats, and the last who entered the lifeboat cut the remaining boat and leave the ship. The narrator is on deck; his family shelter below.

Forgetting the passengers, the ship's company crowded into the lifeboats, and the last who entered cut the ropes to leave the ship, the last of the seamen spring into her and push off, regardless of my cries and entreaties that we might be allowed to share their slender chance of preserving their lives. My voice was drowned in the howling of the blast, and even had the crew wished it, the return of the boat was impossible, for the waves were mountain-high.

Casting my eyes despairingly around, I became gradually aware that our position was by no means hopeless, inasmuch as the ship, though partly shattered, was so jammed between the rocks that it did not go to pieces. As I raised my eyes, I could make out, through rents in the vaporous curtain, a line of rocky coast, rugged and, as it was, my heart bounded towards it as a sign of help in the hour of need.

Yet the sense of our lonely and forsaken condition weighed heavily upon me, as I returned to my family, constraining myself to say with a smile, 'Courage, dear ones! Although our good ship will never sail more, she is so placed that our cabin will remain above water, and tomorrow, if the wind and waves abate, I see no reason why we should not be able to get ashore.'

These few words had an immediate effect on the spirits of my children, for my family has always had an implicit faith in my assurances. The boys at once regarded our problematical chance as a happy certainty, and began to enjoy the relief from the violent pitching and rolling of the vessel.

passages.title: The Storm Lantern
passages.text:
Ella discovered the lantern in the shed behind her grandmother’s house. The door creaked as she pushed it open, releasing a smell of dust and old wood. Light slipped through the cracked window panes, settling on forgotten tools and spider webs stretched thin like silver threads.

The lantern sat on a shelf at the back, its glass cloudy and its metal frame cold to the touch. As Ella wiped away the grime with her sleeve, she noticed a small star-shaped mark engraved along the rim. It seemed to glow faintly, though she could not tell why.

Outside, the sky darkened. Clouds rolled in quickly, piling up like heavy blankets. The air felt tight, and the wind whispered through the trees, bending their branches until leaves scattered across the path. Thunder rumbled somewhere far away.

Ella hesitated. She had been told not to wander during storms, yet something about the lantern made her feel braver. Holding it firmly, she stepped outside. A flash of lightning lit the garden, and for a moment the star on the lantern shone brightly.

Ella did not understand the lantern’s purpose, but as the rain began to fall, she felt certain it was meant to guide her through the storm.

passages.title: The Long Walk Home
passages.text:
Sam tightened his grip on the straps of his rucksack as he stepped through the school gates. The playground noise faded behind him, replaced by the hum of traffic and the distant bark of a dog. Normally, he walked home with his friends, but today he had chosen the longer route.

The pavement narrowed as he moved away from the main road. Houses stood closer together here, their windows dark and silent. Sam slowed his pace, listening to the sound of his shoes against the concrete. Each step echoed louder than he expected.

He reached into his pocket and felt the folded piece of paper. The edges were soft now, worn from being unfolded and refolded throughout the day. Sam stopped beneath a streetlight and stared at the ground. He knew what the note said without reading it again.

A bus roared past, making him jump. Sam took a deep breath and continued walking. By the time he reached the corner shop, his shoulders had relaxed slightly. The light from the shop window spilled onto the pavement, and for the first time since leaving school, Sam allowed himself a small smile.

passages.title: Why Do Volcanoes Erupt?
passages.text:
A volcano is an opening in the Earth’s surface through which molten rock, ash and gases can escape. This molten rock, known as magma, lies beneath the Earth’s crust and is under extreme pressure.

As heat builds up, gases trapped in the magma begin to expand. Eventually, the pressure becomes so great that the magma is forced upwards. When it reaches the surface, an eruption occurs.

Some eruptions are gentle, allowing lava to flow slowly down the sides of a volcano. Others are violent and explosive, sending ash clouds high into the atmosphere. These powerful eruptions can change the surrounding landscape and affect weather patterns.

Volcanoes are often found along the edges of tectonic plates. These plates are constantly moving, although very slowly. When they collide or pull apart, magma can rise through cracks in the Earth’s crust, creating volcanoes.

passages.title: Life in the Rainforest
passages.text:
Rainforests are warm, wet forests found near the equator. They receive large amounts of rainfall throughout the year, which allows a wide variety of plants and animals to thrive.

The forest is divided into layers. At the top is the emergent layer, where the tallest trees grow. Below this lies the canopy, which forms a thick green roof over the forest. The understory and forest floor receive much less sunlight.

Many animals have adapted to life in the rainforest. Some have bright colours to warn predators, while others blend in with their surroundings. Plants often have large leaves to collect as much sunlight as possible.

Rainforests play an important role in regulating the Earth’s climate. They absorb carbon dioxide and release oxygen, helping to keep the planet healthy.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: What phrase is the word tablets short for?

markScheme.modelAnswer: tablet computers

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: According to the author, why might using tablets in the classroom be a good idea? Tick two reasons.
choices: They could help students to use mobile phones.
They could help some students to work more quickly.
They could provide new teaching methods.
They could distract students from their work.
answers: They could help some students to work more quickly.
They could provide new teaching methods.

markScheme.modelAnswer: They could help some students to work more quickly; They could provide new teaching methods.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem:
Additionally, there are endless tablet functions which could provide interesting teaching activities.
Which word in this sentence suggests there are many ways in which tablets can be used in the classroom?

markScheme.modelAnswer: endless

questions.subject: reading

…many of our teachers are not as competent and would need to be trained.
What does the word competent mean in this sentence? Tick one box.

markScheme.modelAnswer: capable

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: What does the author say would be frustrating about using tablets?

markScheme.modelAnswer: The tablets breaking (which would also be expensive to fix).

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Using your own words, summarise what the author thinks about using tablets in the classroom.

award.2: two acceptable points, or one point with supporting evidence

markScheme.modelAnswer: Award up to 2 marks. The author sees positives — tablets could help students work faster and provide new teaching methods (interactive games, animations) — but notes concerns about distraction, teachers needing training, and the cost of tablets breaking. Overall the author is cautiously positive and concludes tablets would be a worthwhile investment.

markScheme.modelAnswer: Any one of: Sire / Your Royal Highness / Your Excellency / Your Majesty / Your Joyfulness / Your Wonderfulness

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Write the numbers 1 to 5 in the boxes to put these events from the script into the right order. The first one has been done for you.
items: The page introduces Jeremy to the king.
Jeremy tells the king that the pigs are flying.
The king suggests that Jeremy joins the court jesters.
The page starts to escort Jeremy out of the room.
Jeremy kneels in front of the king.

markScheme.modelAnswer: 1 — page introduces Jeremy; 2 — Jeremy tells about pigs; 3 — king suggests jesters; 4 — page escorts Jeremy out; 5 — Jeremy kneels.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: What do you think might happen next in the play? Mention two things.

award.2: two acceptable points, or one point with supporting evidence

markScheme.modelAnswer: Award 1 mark per plausible prediction. E.g.: Jeremy brings the king to see a flying pig / The king finally believes Jeremy / Jeremy is still threatened with being made a court jester / The king calls his guards / Jeremy and the king go outside to see the pigs.

markScheme.modelAnswer: afternoon

award.2: two acceptable points, or one point with supporting evidence

markScheme.modelAnswer: Award 1 mark per reason (up to 2). E.g.: There had been no rain for months / The waterholes had dried up / The grass was greener in the north / There was more rain in the north.

questions.subject: reading

markScheme.modelAnswer: happy / satisfied / pleased with what he had

questions.subject: reading

Lions should move north with other animals.
Never chase an antelope instead of a hare.

markScheme.modelAnswer: Don't be greedy, or you might end up with nothing.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: At the beginning of the story, why do you think the lion is described as being the king of all he saw? Mention two reasons, using evidence from the rest of the story to explain your answer.

award.3: three acceptable points, or two points with supporting evidence

markScheme.modelAnswer: Award up to 3 marks for two supported points. E.g.: He was physically powerful — 'strong and swift', so he could dominate other animals. / His appearance was regal — 'his mane glinted like a crown', which suggests royalty. / All other animals feared him — 'all the other animals trembled', showing his power over them. / He commanded his territory — 'he strutted around the plains', like a king surveying his kingdom.

E.g.: Activities for children mentioned (Dewa Roman Experience lets kids try on armour; water slides at the Northgate Arena) — parents would know their children would enjoy it. / Free lunch at the Dewa Roman Experience — parents would be attracted by the value. / The wide variety of food options for all ages — 'There's food for all tastes and ages' — suggests the whole family is catered for. / The text calls Chester 'the perfect place for a weekend break' — directly aimed at families.

questions.subject: reading

award.3: three acceptable points, or two points with supporting evidence

markScheme.modelAnswer: Award up to 3 marks covering different phases with text evidence. Before: apprehensive/nervous ('with some apprehension'). During: focused/excited. After: triumphant/proud ('the triumphant brothers had made the history books'). Full marks require reference to the text and cover more than one phase.

markScheme.modelAnswer: Award 1 mark per relevant point with evidence. E.g.: 'clashing claws' — dragons have claws, suggesting the train is powerful; 'thunderous roars' — dragons roar loudly, like the noise of a train; 'lightning speed' — dragons are depicted as fast, like a speeding train; 'hidden lair' — dragons live in lairs, like a train in tunnels; 'hissing breath' — dragons hiss and breathe fire, like steam from a train.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Give one detail that shows the shed had not been used for a long time.

acceptablePoints.point: dust / old wood / spider webs / forgotten tools

markScheme.modelAnswer: dust / old wood / spider webs / forgotten tools

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Which word best describes how the air felt before the storm?

markScheme.modelAnswer: Tight

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Why does Ella hesitate before going outside?

acceptablePoints.point: She had been told not to wander during storms / she knew it was dangerous

markScheme.modelAnswer: She had been told not to wander during storms / she knew it was dangerous

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: What makes Ella feel braver as she steps outside?

acceptablePoints.point: The lantern / holding the lantern / the lantern makes her feel safe

markScheme.modelAnswer: The lantern / holding the lantern / the lantern makes her feel safe

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Which phrase suggests the storm is approaching quickly?
choices: Light slipped through the cracked window panes
The lantern sat on a shelf

markScheme.modelAnswer: Clouds rolled in quickly

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Explain what the author suggests about the lantern at the end of the story.

acceptablePoints.point: It is meant to guide or protect her / it has a special purpose

markScheme.modelAnswer: It is meant to guide or protect her / it has a special purpose

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Why does Sam choose to walk a different way home?
choices: He wants to avoid his friends

markScheme.modelAnswer: He chooses the longer route

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: What does the note in Sam’s pocket suggest about how he is feeling?

acceptablePoints.point: He is worried / nervous / thinking about something important

markScheme.modelAnswer: He is worried / nervous / thinking about something important

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Which word best describes how Sam feels when the bus roars past?

markScheme.modelAnswer: Startled

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Why does Sam relax when he reaches the corner shop?

acceptablePoints.point: It is brighter / feels safer / he is nearly home

markScheme.modelAnswer: It is brighter / feels safer / he is nearly home

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Explain how the author shows Sam becoming more confident by the end of the text.

acceptablePoints.point: He relaxes his shoulders / he smiles / the light makes him feel safer

markScheme.modelAnswer: He relaxes his shoulders / he smiles / the light makes him feel safer

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: What is molten rock beneath the Earth’s crust called?

markScheme.modelAnswer: magma

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: What causes magma to move towards the Earth’s surface?

markScheme.modelAnswer: Pressure from gases

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Give one effect of a powerful volcanic eruption.

acceptablePoints.point: ash clouds / landscape changes / weather changes

markScheme.modelAnswer: ash clouds / landscape changes / weather changes

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Which word best describes tectonic plates?

markScheme.modelAnswer: Slow-moving

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Why are volcanoes often found at the edges of tectonic plates?

acceptablePoints.point: Magma can rise through cracks when plates move

markScheme.modelAnswer: Magma can rise through cracks when plates move

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Where are rainforests usually found?

markScheme.modelAnswer: Near the equator

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Name one layer of the rainforest.

markScheme.modelAnswer: emergent / canopy / understory / forest floor

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Why do plants in the rainforest have large leaves?

markScheme.modelAnswer: To collect sunlight

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: How do rainforests help the Earth’s climate?

acceptablePoints.point: They absorb carbon dioxide / release oxygen

markScheme.modelAnswer: They absorb carbon dioxide / release oxygen

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Explain why rainforests are important for animals.

markScheme.modelAnswer: They provide habitats / food / protection

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: "Amara grabbed her umbrella and pulled her coat tighter as she stepped outside." What is the weather most likely like?

markScheme.modelAnswer: Rainy and cold
markScheme.explanation: Retrieval questions ask you to find clues directly in the text. An umbrella suggests rain; pulling her coat tighter suggests cold. Together they point to rainy and cold.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: "James slammed his book shut and stared at the ceiling." What can you infer about James?
choices: He is confused by the book

markScheme.modelAnswer: He is bored or frustrated
markScheme.explanation: Inference means reading between the lines. Slamming a book shut suggests frustration; staring at the ceiling can suggest boredom or exasperation.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: "The ancient ruins were shrouded in mystery." What does "shrouded" mean here?

markScheme.modelAnswer: Covered or hidden
markScheme.explanation: "Shrouded" means wrapped or covered. A shroud traditionally covers a body. Here it means the ruins are surrounded and hidden by mystery.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: A pupil claims: "The author wants us to feel sorry for the character." Which evidence best supports this?
choices: Quoting words that show the character is happy
Quoting a phrase that shows the character suffers
Describing what happens at the end
questions.answer: Quoting a phrase that shows the character suffers

markScheme.modelAnswer: Quoting a phrase that shows the character suffers
markScheme.explanation: When justifying a view, always quote directly from the text. A phrase that shows suffering (e.g. "she wept silently") directly supports the claim.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Which sentence contains a simile?
choices: The wind howled through the trees.
Her laughter was music to his ears.
His hands were as cold as ice.
The stars danced in the midnight sky.
questions.answer: His hands were as cold as ice.

markScheme.modelAnswer: His hands were as cold as ice.
markScheme.explanation: A simile compares two things using "like" or "as". "As cold as ice" uses "as…as". The others use personification or metaphor.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: An author uses short, punchy sentences in an action scene. What effect does this create?
choices: It slows the reader down to build suspense
It creates a fast pace and excitement
It describes the setting in detail
questions.answer: It creates a fast pace and excitement

markScheme.modelAnswer: It creates a fast pace and excitement
markScheme.explanation: Short sentences speed up the pace of writing. In action scenes, this creates urgency and excitement, making the reader feel the tension of the moment.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: "Peter Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers." This is an example of:

markScheme.modelAnswer: Alliteration
markScheme.explanation: Alliteration is the repetition of the same consonant sound at the start of closely linked words. Here, the "p" sound is repeated throughout.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Which word is closest in meaning to "magnificent"?

markScheme.modelAnswer: Splendid
markScheme.explanation: "Magnificent" means impressively beautiful or grand. "Splendid" means excellent or impressive — the closest synonym. "Ordinary" is the opposite.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Which features suggest a text is a newspaper report?
choices: Once upon a time and a moral

markScheme.modelAnswer: Headline, byline, and columns
markScheme.explanation: Newspaper reports have a headline (title), byline (writer's name), and are written in columns. They use the third person and report facts in a formal style.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Which sentence is a fact?
choices: Maths is the most important subject.

markScheme.modelAnswer: The Earth orbits the Sun.
markScheme.explanation: A fact can be proved true or false. "The Earth orbits the Sun" is scientifically proven. The others are opinions — personal views that people can disagree about.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: "Maya stared at her untouched plate. The smell of her favourite meal filled the room, but she pushed it away." What can you infer about Maya?
choices: She is not hungry, despite it being her favourite meal
She does not like the meal
She is in a hurry to leave
questions.answer: She is not hungry, despite it being her favourite meal

markScheme.modelAnswer: She is not hungry, despite it being her favourite meal
markScheme.explanation: The key clue is "favourite meal" — she normally likes it. Yet she has not eaten and pushes it away, suggesting something is wrong emotionally, not that she dislikes the food.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: "The politician gave an eloquent speech." What does "eloquent" mean?

markScheme.modelAnswer: Fluent and persuasive
markScheme.explanation: "Eloquent" describes speech or writing that is fluent, expressive, and persuasive. An eloquent speaker chooses words well and communicates clearly and effectively.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: What is the effect of repetition in a poem?
choices: It emphasises an idea and creates rhythm
It makes the poem harder to understand
It shows the poet has run out of ideas
questions.answer: It emphasises an idea and creates rhythm

markScheme.modelAnswer: It emphasises an idea and creates rhythm
markScheme.explanation: Repetition draws attention to a key word or idea. It also creates rhythm and can build emotion or emphasise a theme throughout the poem.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: An author describes a forest as "dark, silent, and still". What atmosphere does this create?

markScheme.modelAnswer: Threatening or eerie
markScheme.explanation: Words like "dark", "silent", and "still" suggest something ominous or unsettling. Authors carefully choose adjectives to create a specific mood or atmosphere.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: What is a metaphor?
choices: When words start with the same sound
A direct comparison, saying something IS something else
Words that sound like what they describe
questions.answer: A direct comparison, saying something IS something else

markScheme.modelAnswer: A direct comparison, saying something IS something else
markScheme.explanation: A metaphor says something IS something else (e.g. "Life is a journey"). A simile uses "like" or "as". Alliteration repeats consonants; onomatopoeia imitates sounds.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: When a question asks you to "explain", what should you do?
choices: Copy a sentence from the text
Give your opinion about the text
Give reasons and use evidence from the text
Write a summary of the whole passage
questions.answer: Give reasons and use evidence from the text

markScheme.modelAnswer: Give reasons and use evidence from the text
markScheme.explanation: "Explain" questions want reasons supported by evidence. Always quote from the text and then comment on what the quotation shows.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Why might an author start a story with dialogue?
choices: To describe the setting in detail
To immediately engage the reader in the action
To introduce all characters at once
questions.answer: To immediately engage the reader in the action

markScheme.modelAnswer: To immediately engage the reader in the action
markScheme.explanation: Starting with dialogue drops the reader straight into the action. It creates immediacy and makes the reader want to find out who is speaking and why.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: "The scientist's discovery was groundbreaking." What does "groundbreaking" mean?

markScheme.modelAnswer: Completely new and innovative
markScheme.explanation: "Groundbreaking" means revolutionary or entirely new — it "breaks new ground" like a plough cutting into soil for the first time.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: "The waves crashed and roared against the cliff." What technique is used?

markScheme.modelAnswer: Onomatopoeia
markScheme.explanation: "Crashed" and "roared" are onomatopoeic words — they sound like the actions they describe. Onomatopoeia makes writing more vivid and immersive.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Which type of question asks you to find information stated directly in the text?

markScheme.modelAnswer: Retrieval
markScheme.explanation: Retrieval questions ask you to find information that is explicitly stated in the text — you just need to locate it. Inference requires you to "read between the lines".

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: What does "personification" mean?
choices: Giving human qualities to non-human things
Words that sound like what they describe
questions.answer: Giving human qualities to non-human things

markScheme.modelAnswer: Giving human qualities to non-human things
markScheme.explanation: Personification gives human feelings or actions to objects, animals, or ideas. Example: "The wind whispered through the trees" — the wind cannot actually whisper.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: "She was reluctant to speak in front of the class." What does "reluctant" mean?

markScheme.modelAnswer: Unwilling or hesitant
markScheme.explanation: "Reluctant" means unwilling or not wanting to do something. It comes from the Latin reluctari, meaning to struggle against.

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Which is a good example of point-evidence-explain (PEE)?
choices: The character is sad. He is in the story.
The character feels isolated. The author writes he 'stood alone'. This shows he has no one nearby.
The story is interesting and I liked it.
The author uses adjectives to describe the character.
questions.answer: The character feels isolated. The author writes he 'stood alone'. This shows he has no one nearby.

markScheme.modelAnswer: The character feels isolated. The author writes he 'stood alone'. This shows he has no one nearby.
markScheme.explanation: PEE = Point, Evidence (a quote), Explain. The second option has all three: point (isolated), evidence (quote), explanation (no one nearby).

questions.subject: reading
questions.stem: Which feature is typical of a persuasive text?

markScheme.modelAnswer: Rhetorical questions and emotive language
markScheme.explanation: Persuasive texts use rhetorical questions ("Surely everyone deserves a chance?"), emotive language, facts, opinions, and repetition to convince the reader.
